perturbation.original_text: Purpose means [STATE THE PURPOSE, FOR EXAMPLE, THE EVALUATION OR ESTABLISHMENT OF A COLLABORATION IN RESPECT OF A PARTICULAR PROJECT ].
perturbation.changed_text: Purpose means the exchange of information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the definition of 'Purpose' from a specific project to 'the exchange of information' significantly broadens its scope. This introduces a contradiction in Section 2.1(b), which restricts the use of Confidential Information 'in any way except for the Purpose.' The broad new definition of 'Purpose' weakens the restriction because any use related to 'the exchange of information' could be argued as permissible, undermining the confidentiality obligations intended in the original, narrower definition of 'Purpose'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Recipient shall keep the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information confidential and, except with the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party, shall: (a) Warrants and undertake that it is solely responsible for ensuring that the Personal Data is processed by it in accordance with the Data Protection Act 1998 (“the Act”) from the date that it is received from the Disclosing Party; (b) Not use or exploit the Confidential Information in any way except for the Purpose. If the Recipient proposes to use or process the Personal Data for a purpose other than the Purpose, the Recipient shall ensure that, prior to using or processing the Personal Data in such manner, it will ensure that such use or process is in compliance with the Act; (c) Not disclose or make available the Confidential Information in whole or in part to any third party, except as expressly permitted by this Agreement; and (d) Not copy, reduce to writing or otherwise record the Confidential Information except as strictly necessary for the Purpose (and any such copies, reductions to writing and records shall be the property of the Disclosing Party).
perturbation.justification: The change to the definition of 'Purpose' from a specific project to 'the exchange of information' creates a contradiction with the contradicted text. Section 2.1(b) restricts the use of Confidential Information 'in any way except for the Purpose.' The new broad definition of 'Purpose' allows for wider use of confidential information, undermining the confidentiality obligations that the original definition sought to impose. This directly conflicts with the recipient's obligation to keep the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information confidential and not use it except for the originally intended purpose.

perturbation.original_text: 2.2 The Recipient may disclose the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information to those of its Representatives who need to know this Confidential Information for the Purpose, provided that: (a) It informs its Representatives of the confidential nature of the Confidential Information before disclosure; (b) It procures that its Representatives shall, in relation to any Confidential Information disclosed to them, comply with this Agreement as if they were SAMPLE4 the Recipient and, if the Disclosing Party so requests, procure that any relevant Representative enters into a confidentiality agreement with the Disclosing Party on terms equivalent to those contained in this Agreement ; and (c) It keeps a written record of these Representatives, and it shall at all times be liable for the failure of any Representative to comply with the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.2 The Recipient may disclose the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information to its Representatives.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the conditions (a), (b) and (c) under which the Recipient can disclose Confidential Information to its Representatives, the clause now broadly permits disclosure without any safeguards. This creates a direct contradiction with the core principle of maintaining confidentiality established in Section 2.1, and specifically weakens the obligation in 2.1(c) that prohibits disclosing Confidential Information to any third party.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Recipient shall keep the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information confidential and, except with the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party, shall: (a) Warrants and undertake that it is solely responsible for ensuring that the Personal Data is processed by it in accordance with the Data Protection Act 1998 (“the Act”) from the date that it is received from the Disclosing Party; (b) Not use or exploit the Confidential Information in any way except for the Purpose. If the Recipient proposes to use or process the Personal Data for a purpose other than the Purpose, the Recipient shall ensure that, prior to using or processing the Personal Data in such manner, it will ensure that such use or process is in compliance with the Act; (c) Not disclose or make available the Confidential Information in whole or in part to any third party, except as expressly permitted by this Agreement; and (d) Not copy, reduce to writing or otherwise record the Confidential Information except as strictly necessary for the Purpose (and any such copies, reductions to writing and records shall be the property of the Disclosing Party).
perturbation.justification: The original text in 2.2 had conditions (a), (b), and (c) that needed to be met for the Recipient to disclose Confidential Information to its Representatives. The changed text removes these conditions, allowing for broad disclosure. This directly contradicts section 2.1(c), which states that the Recipient shall 'Not disclose or make available the Confidential Information in whole or in part to any third party, except as expressly permitted by this Agreement.' Since the changed text in 2.2 removes the restrictions for disclosing to Representatives, it now directly conflicts with the restriction in 2.1(c) that requires permission, making the change a strong in-text contradiction by omission.

perturbation.original_text: 2.4 The Recipient shall establish and maintain adequate security measures (including any reasonable security measures proposed by the Disclosing party from time to time) to safeguard the Confidential Information from unauthorised access or use.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.4 The Recipient should consider security measures to safeguard the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall establish and maintain adequate security measures' to 'should consider security measures' makes the implementation of security measures discretionary rather than mandatory. This directly contradicts the overarching obligation of confidentiality established in Section 2.1, because without mandatory security measures, the risk of unauthorized access and disclosure of Confidential Information significantly increases, making the initial confidentiality promise less enforceable. By weakening the obligation to safeguard the confidential information, this now affects enforceability in 2.1.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Recipient shall keep the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information confidential and, except with the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party, shall: (a) Warrants and undertake that it is solely responsible for ensuring that the Personal Data is processed by it in accordance with the Data Protection Act 1998 (“the Act”) from the date that it is received from the Disclosing Party; (b) Not use or exploit the Confidential Information in any way except for the Purpose. If the Recipient proposes to use or process the Personal Data for a purpose other than the Purpose, the Recipient shall ensure that, prior to using or processing the Personal Data in such manner, it will ensure that such use or process is in compliance with the Act; (c) Not disclose or make available the Confidential Information in whole or in part to any third party, except as expressly permitted by this Agreement; and (d) Not copy, reduce to writing or otherwise record the Confidential Information except as strictly necessary for the Purpose (and any such copies, reductions to writing and records shall be the property of the Disclosing Party).
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates security measures ('shall establish and maintain'). The changed text weakens this to a suggestion ('should consider'). The contradicted text obligates the recipient to keep confidential information secure ('shall keep the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information confidential'). The change, by making security measures discretionary, directly contradicts the mandatory obligation to keep information confidential, because it lowers the standard of care required.

perturbation.original_text: 5.2 Each Recipient shall indemnify and keep fully indemnified the Disclosing Party at all times against all liabilities, costs (including legal costs on an indemnity basis), expenses, damages and losses (including any direct, indirect or consequential losses, loss of profit, loss of reputation and all interest, penalties and other reasonable costs and expenses suffered or incurred by the Disclosing Party) arising from any breach of this Agreement by the Recipient and from the actions or omissions of any Representative of the Recipient.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.2 The Recipient may be liable for damages arising from a breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing the mandatory 'shall indemnify and keep fully indemnified' with the discretionary 'may be liable for damages' significantly weakens the Disclosing Party's protection in case of a breach by the Recipient. The original clause provides a comprehensive indemnity covering all types of losses and costs. The altered clause only mentions potential liability for 'damages', leaving the scope and extent of that liability undefined and subject to interpretation. This change is in contradiction to Section 4.4 'The Recipient acknowledges that damages alone would not be an adequate remedy for the breach of any of the provisions of this Agreement'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Recipient acknowledges that damages alone would not be an adequate remedy for the breach of any of the provisions of this Agreement. Accordingly, without prejudice to any other rights and remedies it may have, the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to the granting of equitable relief (including without limitation injunctive relief) concerning any threatened or actual breach of any of the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates indemnification for all losses, while the changed text makes the Recipient only 'liable for damages', a much narrower scope. This contradicts the Contradicted Text, which states that 'damages alone would not be an adequate remedy'. The change from mandatory indemnification to discretionary liability for damages directly conflicts with the acknowledgment that damages are insufficient, creating a strong in-text contradiction. The explanation provided also highlights this contradiction, further supporting the 'YES' assessment.

perturbation.original_text: 13.2 The parties irrevocably agree that the courts of England and Wales shall have non- exclusive jurisdiction to settle any dispute or claim that arises out of or in connection SAMPLE8 with this Agreement or its subject matter or formation (including non-contractual disputes or claims).
perturbation.changed_text: 13.2 Disputes arising from this Agreement shall be subject to negotiation.
perturbation.explanation: By replacing the clause that establishes the jurisdiction of the courts of England and Wales with 'Disputes arising from this Agreement shall be subject to negotiation,' the agreement becomes internally contradictory. The original clause provides a clear legal avenue for resolving disputes. The new clause only mentions negotiation, leaving it unclear whether parties are still able to seek legal recourse if negotiation fails. This is in contradiction with Section 7.2 'Each party agrees that it shall have no remedies in respect of any representation or warranty (whether made innocently or negligently) that is not set out in this Agreement.'
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each party agrees that it shall have no remedies in respect of any representation or warranty (whether made innocently or negligently) that is not set out in this Agreement. Each party agrees that its only liability in respect of those representations and warranties that are set out in this Agreement (whether made innocently or negligently) shall be for breach of contract.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies that the courts of England and Wales have jurisdiction over disputes. The changed text replaces this with a requirement for negotiation, removing the clear legal avenue for dispute resolution. This contradicts Section 7.2, which stipulates that remedies are limited to breach of contract claims set out in the Agreement. By removing the jurisdiction clause, the availability and process for these remedies become uncertain, creating a contradiction. The change alters the process of remedies, directly conflicting with the clause in section 7.2
